He complained under Article 6 § 1 of the Convention that the Independent Administrative Panel had failed to hold a hearing. Such a hearing would have been necessary because his appeal was not limited to points of law. He had also criticised the District Administrative Authority’s assessment of evidence. Therefore an oral hearing would have been necessary. In such a hearing the Independent Administrative Panel could have properly assessed this evidence, which had already been obtained at first instance, as well as fresh evidence.

On 13 July 1995 the Constitutional Court declined to deal with the applicant’s complaint on the ground that it did not have sufficient prospects of success. Upon a request filed by the applicant on 10 August 1995, the Constitutional Court transferred the case to the Administrative Court (Verwaltungsgerichtshof).

On 23 February 1996 the Administrative Court, relying on Section 33a of the Administrative Court Act (Verwaltungsgerichtshofgesetz), declined to deal with the applicant’s case, finding that it did not raise important legal issues. On 10 May 1996 this decision was served on the applicant’s lawyer.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 20089/06) against the Republic of Austria lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an Austrian national, Mr Hans Herbert Puchstein (“the applicant”), on 5 May 2006.

The applicant was represented by Mr M. Lechner, a lawyer practising in Lochau. The Austrian Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Ambassador F. Trauttmansdorff, Head of the International Law Department at the Federal Ministry for European and International Affairs.

The applicant alleged that the proceedings concerning his doctor’s fees had not been dealt with within a reasonable time and that the Regional Appeals Commission which dealt with his case had not been impartial and independent.

On 7 May 2008 the President of the First Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant lives in St Oswald.

The applicant is a medical practitioner. He has an individual contract (Einzelvertrag) with the Lower Austrian Health Insurance Board (Niederösterreichische Gebietskrankenkasse).

In two submissions to the Joint Arbitration Committee (Paritätische Schiedskommission) dated 7 September 1998 and 5 October 1998, the applicant demanded payment for laboratory tests carried out in the first and second quarters of 1998. The claims against the Lower Austrian Health Insurance Board amounted to 8,022.41 euros (EUR) and EUR 6,603.81, respectively.

The Lower Austrian Health Insurance Board claimed that the applicant had not participated in the test series for quality checking of laboratory parameters in 1997, and refused to pay for the tests.

The applicant argued that he had participated in the quality checks in previous years with positive results and had – as previously – submitted the relevant samples for the period in question.

The Joint Arbitration Committee held an oral hearing and on 25 February 1999 it dismissed the applicant’s claim of 7 September 1998.

The applicant appealed against that decision to the Regional Appeals Commission (Landesberufungskommission).

On 17 May 1999 the applicant filed an application for transfer of jurisdiction (Devolutionsantrag), requesting a decision on his submission of 5 October 1998 from the Regional Appeals Commission, as the Joint Arbitration Committee had failed to decide within the statutory six-month time-limit.

The Regional Appeals Commission held a hearing and rejected both of the applicant’s claims. The decision was dated 24 August 1999, and was served on the applicant on 7 July 2000.

The applicant complained about this decision to the Constitutional Court (Verfassungsgerichtshof). On 27 November 2000 the Constitutional Court set aside the decision for violation of the right to a fair hearing before an independent and impartial tribunal established by law. It noted that one of the assessors of the Regional Appeals Commission had negotiated the clause on remuneration for laboratory tests on behalf of the Lower Austrian Medical Association (Ärztekammer für Niederösterreich), and thus the tribunal could not be regarded as impartial and/or independent.

An amendment to the Social Insurance Act (Allgemeines Sozialversicherungsgesetz) entered into force on 1 September 2002; it provided for changes in the composition of, inter alia, the Regional Appeals Commission.

The newly composed Regional Appeals Commission held a hearing on 15 May 2002, during which the applicant gave evidence.

On 24 March 2003 the applicant requested that the decision be served on him. As the Regional Appeals Commission did not react, the applicant repeated his demand on 18 July 2003.

The applicant was summoned to another hearing by a newly composed Regional Appeals Commission on 26 November 2003. During that hearing the entire subject was examined anew. Apparently the Regional Appeals Commission reached a decision on the same day. It dismissed the applicant’s claims, finding that he had failed to participate in the required tests and had therefore not complied with the conditions for payment of the laboratory tests. The Regional Appeals Commission did not find any reasons to doubt the lawfulness of the underlying provision of the Remuneration Regulation (Honorarordnung). The decision was served on the applicant’s counsel on 30 November 2004.

On 15 December 2004 the applicant complained to the Constitutional Court claiming that the Regional Appeals Commission lacked independence and impartiality. The former rejected the applicant’s complaint in a decision of 27 September 2005. It observed that the members of the Regional Appeals Commission were not bound by any instructions. They were independent and impartial unless special circumstances, for instance the fact that one member of the Regional Appeals Commission had been involved in the negotiation of the general agreements, gave rise to legitimate doubts about that member’s independence and impartiality. Such special circumstances had not been shown by the applicant in the present case. The mere fact that the assessors were members of Regional Medial Associations or Regional Health Insurance Boards which had provisions with the same content in their general agreements, did not suffice to cast doubt on the independence and impartiality of the Regional Appeals Commission. The decision was served on counsel on 29 November 2005.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 42967/98) against the Republic of Austria lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an Austrian national, Mr Philemon Löffelmann (“the applicant”), on 9 July 1998.

The applicant was represented by Mr R. Kohlhofer, a lawyer practising in Vienna. The Austrian Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr F. Trauttmansdorff, Head of the International Law Department at the Federal Ministry for European and International Affairs.

The applicant alleged that he had been discriminated against in the exercise of his rights under Articles 4 and 9 of the Convention on the ground of his religion as he was liable for military or alternative civilian service whereas members of recognised religious societies holding religious functions comparable to his functions were exempted.

The application was transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

By a decision of 1 February 2005 the Court declared the application partly admissible.

Neither the applicant nor the Government filed further written observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1976 and lives in Maissau.

Upon his baptism on 9 July 1994 he became a member of the Jehovah’s Witnesses in Austria, within which he assumed the function of a preacher or “regular pioneer” (Prediger, allgemeiner Pionier) and, since 27 November 1996, a deacon or “ministerial servant” (Diakon, Dienstamtgehilfe). In this function he assisted the clerical work of elders of the Jehovah’s Witnesses.

On 17 November 1994 the Lower Austrian Military Authority (Militärkommando) found that the applicant was fit to perform military service. On 3 July 1995 the applicant started his military service; however, on 1 August 1995 he was discharged following a military medical expert opinion that had found him unfit for service.

On 28 September 1995 the Lower Austrian Military Authority issued a conscription order (Stellungsbescheid) in respect of the applicant, ordering him to undergo another examination as to his ability to perform military service pursuant to section 24(8) of the Military Service Act (Wehrgesetz), as in force at the relevant time. As to the applicant’s argument that he should be exempted from military service under section 24(3) of the Military Service Act, it noted that the applicant was not a member of a recognised religious society.

The applicant appealed against that order, claiming in particular that he should be dispensed from military service since he performed a function within the Jehovah’s Witnesses which was equivalent to that of members of a recognised religious society who were exempted from military service under section 24(3) of the Military Service Act. To restrict such a privilege to members of recognised religious societies was not objectively justified and was therefore in breach of the Federal Constitution.

On 16 November 1995 the Federal Minister for Defence (Bundesminister für Landesverteidigung) dismissed the applicant’s appeal and confirmed the lower authority’s decision.

On 8 January 1996 the applicant lodged a complaint with the Constitutional Court (Verfassungsgerichtshof), requesting it to repeal the wording “recognised religious societies” in section 24(3) of the Military Service Act.

On 1 December 1997 the Constitutional Court refused to deal with the applicant’s complaint for lack of prospects of success. It referred to an earlier decision by which it had found that the obligation to perform military or civilian service raised no concerns as regards compliance with Article 9 of the Convention.

On 26 March 1998 the Administrative Court (Verwaltungs-gerichtshof) dismissed the applicant’s complaint. It noted that the applicant had solely complained about section 24(3) of the Military Service Act in conjunction with the 1998 Act on the Legal Status of Registered Religious Communities (Bundesgesetz über die Rechtspersönlichkeit von religiösen Bekenntnisgemeinschaften – hereafter referred to as the “1998 Act”), which had entered into force on 10 January 1998. However, the Administrative Court had to limit its examination of the legality of the drafting order to the legal situation at the time when the order had been issued. Referring to the case-law quoted by the Constitutional Court, it found no concerns as regards the legality of the drafting order and therefore also no indication to institute proceedings to review constitutionality (Gesetzesprüfungsverfahren) as proposed by the applicant.

On 14 May 1998 the Lower Austrian Military Authority issued another conscription order for an examination of the applicant’s fitness to perform military service.

On 19 May 1998 the applicant lodged a complaint with the Constitutional Court against the order. He submitted in particular that by virtue of the 1998 Act, the Jehovah’s Witnesses had been granted the status of a “registered religious community”. However, the ten-year period for a successful application for recognition under the Recognition Act (Anerkennungsgesetz), newly introduced by section 11 of the 1998 Act, lacked objective justification. Furthermore, it precluded any recognition for the following ten years. Since section 24(3) of the Military Service Act referred to “recognised religious societies” and restricted exemption from military service to members of recognised religious societies, the applicant again requested the Constitutional Court to revoke this limitation and also to revoke the ten-year period prescribed in section 11 of the 1998 Act.

On 8 June 1998 the Constitutional Court refused to deal with the complaint for lack of prospects of success. It further held that the provision of the 1998 Act referred to was not directly applicable to the case at issue.

Subsequently, the applicant filed a request for recognition as a conscientious objector (Zivildiensterklärung), which was granted.

Between 1 February 1999 and 31 January 2000 he performed his civilian service in a social institution.

On 1 February 2000 the applicant joined the “Religious Order of the Jehovah’s Witnesses” (Orden der Sondervollzeitdiener der Zeugen Jehovas), where he lived and worked as a preacher (Bethelmitarbeiter).

In February 2001 he left the order and continued to work as a preacher and deacon.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 41444/98) against the Republic of Austria lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an Austrian national, Mr Otto Hennig (“the applicant”), on 20 March 1998.

The applicant was represented by Amhof, Damian and Partners, a law firm practising in Vienna. The Austrian Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Ambassador H. Winkler, Head of the International Law Department at the Federal Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged that the criminal proceedings against him lasted unreasonably long in breach of Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court).

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section (Rule 52 § 1). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision of 23 May 2002 the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1938 and lives in Oberwart/Austria. He is an auditor and tax consultant (Wirtschaftstreuhänder).

In December 1989, the Salzburg Tax Office (Finanzamt), in the course of investigations into a large scale fraud relating to the “WEB/IMMAG” group, instituted criminal proceedings against 97 persons, requesting them to submit in writing their comments as suspects.

On 14 December 1989 the applicant, who in his professional capacity assisted the “WEB/IMMAG” group, wrote a letter to the Oberwart Tax Office. Therein he asked that his income tax declarations for the years 1985 to 1987 be corrected, so that the losses declared be cancelled, in particular ATS 541,585 for the year 1985; ATS 1 million for 1986 and ATS 220,000 for 1987. He further asked that following a re-calculation of his income, new tax assessment orders be issued.

Investigations by the Tax Authorities against the applicant for tax evasion

On 27 and 28 December 1989 the Salzburg Tax Office informed the applicant that he was suspected of tax evasion in that he had acted as a sham holder of shares in three cases concerning the years 1985–87. It further invited him to submit his comments in writing.

After the applicant's request of 15 January 1990 for an extension of the time-limit had been granted, he submitted his comments on 31 January 1990.

Until 29 October 1992 the Salzburg Tax Office for the Audit of Large-scale Companies (Großbetriebsprüfung) examined the “WEB/IMMAG” group.

On 22 March 1993 the Salzburg Tax Office requested the Oberwart Tax Office to transmit the applicant's tax file in order to determine the amount of evaded taxes.

On 17 May 1993 the Oberwart Tax Office replied to the Salzburg Tax Office that the file could not be transferred because of pending investigations.

Following another request for transfer of the file by the Salzburg Tax Office on 23 July 1993, the Oberwart Tax Office, on 24 January 1994, replied that the file had been sent to the Vienna Tax Office for the Audit of Large-scale Companies and could only be transmitted after these investigations had been completed.

Upon the Salzburg Tax Office's request of 28 January 1994, the Vienna Tax Office for the Audit of Large-scale Companies transferred the applicant's file on 25 May 1994.

On 3 June 1994 the Salzburg Tax Office informed the Salzburg Public Prosecutor's Office of the result of its investigations and requested that the applicant be prosecuted for tax evasion. It noted that the losses declared by the applicant for the years 1985–1989 (ATS 541,585 for the year 1985, ATS 1 million for 1986 and ATS 220,000 for 1987) resulted in tax evasion of ATS 327,016 for the year 1985, ATS 603,552 for 1986 and ATS 148,821 for 1987. Thus, the total amount of evaded taxes was ATS 1,079,389.

On 8 August 1994, when interrogated by the Oberwart District Court, the applicant stated that he wished to submit his comments in writing directly to the Salzburg Regional Court by 31 August 1994. On that date and on 30 September 1994, he requested extensions of the time-limit as he had fallen ill. On 27 January 1995 he submitted his comments and observations in writing.

2. Court proceedings against the applicant

On 9 February 1995 the Public Prosecutor's Office preferred a bill of indictment against the applicant charging him with tax evasion of ATS 1,079,389 in that he had made false statements of losses in his income tax forms between 1985 and 1987 (ATS 541,585 for 1985; ATS 1 million for 1986 and ATS 220,000 for 1987). The bill of indictment comprised eleven pages.

On 31 March 1995, the presiding judge of the chamber dealing with the applicant's case informed the President of the Regional Court that he considered himself biased since he had been the deputy investigating judge in proceedings against other accused relating to the same case. On 21 June 1995, the President decided nevertheless that the presiding judge should not withdraw from the case.

On 4 September 1995 the applicant requested that the trial scheduled for 13 September 1995 be adjourned. This request was granted by the court.

On 22 November 1995 the Regional Court convicted the applicant of tax evasion, pursuant to Section 33 § 1 of the Code of Tax Offences (Finanzstrafgesetz). As regards the applicant's argument that he could not be punished because his letter of 14 December 1989 constituted “self-denunciation” of a tax offence resulting in exemption from punishment, the court observed that it had not been made in time because the tax authorities had already discovered the offence, and it had not been sufficiently detailed for the purposes of a “self-denunciation”.

On 29 October 1996 the written version of the judgment, comprising twelve pages, was served on the applicant's counsel. Thereupon, on 26 November 1996, the applicant filed a plea of nullity (Nichtigkeitsbeschwerde) and requested that the transcripts of the trial be corrected. On 4 February 1997, after having obtained various statements, the Salzburg Regional Court corrected the transcripts.

On 28 May 1997 the Procurator General (Generalprokurator) submitted his observations on the applicant's plea of nullity.

On 25 June 1997 the Supreme Court scheduled the hearing on the plea of nullity for 29 July 1997. On that day the Supreme Court dismissed the applicant's plea of nullity. This decision was pronounced orally. On 2 October 1997 the written version of the judgment, consisting of twelve pages, was served on the applicant's counsel.

1.0: The applicant, Mr. X, sought justice at the European Court of Human Rights in a landmark case that would set a precedent for the protection of the right to a peaceful dwelling for individuals with disabilities. The events in question took place in a small town where Mr. X resided, and a local authority's denial of his application to renovate his house to make it wheelchair-accessible was met with fierce resistance from the community.

"The applicant, a resident of Denmark, alleged that the state of the United Kingdom had breached her right to life as enshrined in Article 2 of the European Convention by failing to provide adequate healthcare facilities, leading to the death of her mother who suffered from diabetic ulceration and other related health issues; the Court notes that the failure was not due to any intentional act by the UK, but instead a result of systemic and structural issues within the healthcare system."

The president of the court delivered her judgment in the case of Vasilescu v. Romania, ruling that the Romanian government's policy of "rehabilitation" of convicted individuals was incompatible with Article 7 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which guarantees the right to fair trial, by allowing prosecutors and judges to " Rehabilitate offenders without their consent."

The European Court of Human Rights has reaffirmed its stance on the freedom of expression, stating that arbitrary detention is only permissible if there are reasonable grounds. In a recent decision, the court found that the actions of the authorities were in line with Article 16 of the European Convention, which allows for deprivation of liberty in certain circumstances. However, the court emphasized that the detention should be proportionate to the risk to national security and that all necessary measures should be taken to prevent further threats.

"The High Court of the European Union found in favor of Zdzisław Paweł S, a Polish national, whose right to free and peaceful assembly had been infringed by the Polish Ministry of Internal Affairs. According to the judgment issued on February 27, 2020, the court acknowledged that the demonstration on Kryńska Street in Białystok on November 8, 2017, had not posed an immediate threat of public security or disturbance."

The question before the Court focused on the incompatibility of a temporary placement order imposed on a prisoner with Article 13 of the European Convention, given its failure to determine the facts and grounds of the contested conduct.

The Court of Grand Chamber, Acting pursuant to Article 54 of the Convention and Rules 76 to 81, the European Court held that the requirement that Ukraine prove that the Russian government failed to rehabilitate it must pass through a proper instance prior to the examination of the admissibility of the complaint.

"In the case of Zhelyabska and The State of Ukraine, the European Court of Human Rights underscored the imperative of domestic authorities recognizing and implementing national laws to prevent the refoulement of individuals fleeing conflict zones, with a recent national law on asyl...

On March 17, 2022, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights ruled in the case of Smirnyi v. Russia, a challenge to the country's domestic laws permitting law enforcement agencies to monitor and record private communications without prior authorization under the guise of "for the purpose of counter-terrorism and counter-extremism."

"In considering the right to privacy as an essential aspect of human dignity, the ECHR has consistently established that certain information, while not actionable, may still cause substantial harm to an individual's reputation upon its disclosure."

**Synthetic court case transcript:** The applicants, Mr. John Smith and Ms. Jane Doe, brought a complaint before the European Court of Human Rights, claiming that the authorities had unfairly denied them their right to free speech under Article 10 of the European Convention. The applicants alleged that they had been arrested and detained after publishing an editorial in a local newspaper, criticizing the government's handling of a recent economic crisis. Despite submitting their application eight months prior to the incident, the police had failed to respond, leading to a significant delay in the investigation. The applicants sought damages for the distress, reputational harm, and the undue restriction on their freedom of expression. The ECHR ultimately found that the authorities had indeed breached the applicants' Article 10 rights, and the European Court of Human Rights ruled in their favor, ordering the state to provide compensation for the violations.

"In 2018, the European Court of Human Rights issued a landmark ruling in the case of Yasin Kayi v. Cyprus, finding a substantial probability that the decision by Cyprus to restrict the public availability of online content related to historical events, as stipulated in the Press and Broadcasting Services Bill, was in breach of Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights."

This landmark decision effectively clarified that "effective access to a court" encompasses the entirety of the trial process, not merely the presence of a lawyer during crucial stages. "It is essential to acknowledge that the European Court of Human Rights has consistently employed a nuanced approach in resolving cases involving the right to a fair trial, emphasizing the importance of balancing individual rights with the legitimate interests of the State in maintaining public order; as seen in the precedent set forth in the case of McElhinney v. the United Kingdom, where the court held that an examination before a single judge could be sufficient where the applicant's rights were not fundamentally breached, and yet the margin of appreciation afforded to the respondent State must be proportional to the measures taken to prevent harm to a third party."

"The European Court of Human Rights has reiterated its commitment to protecting the rights of all individuals whose lives are torn apart by traumatic experiences, including those who have endured inhumane treatment and inexcusable violence at the hands of state authorities, leaving an indelible mark that can only be erased by the granting of redress and justice."

No case was brought before the European Court of Human Rights in a capacity in which the applicant had to fear they might be subject to an arbitrary measure to give their information of fact and to use or disclose it.

"The ECHR's jurisdiction to hear appeals against convictions upheld, yet it remained that judges must weigh the weight of the facts, evidence, and relevant legal principles, striking a delicate balance between human rights and societal norms, all within the well established framework governing individual rights in Europe."

"The ECHR has recognized that the right to a fair trial is not only a fundamental right, but also an essential component of a just and equitable society; however, it has also been demonstrated that inequality of arms between litigants can hinder this objective, leading to diminished access to justice for those at the periphery of the system; therefore, it is imperative to address this disparity through targeted reform, focusing on measures that ensure equal participation and representation."

Here is a synthetic court opinion similar to the style found in the European Court of Human Rights' judgements: "The applicant, a 32-year-old Lithuanian national, was at the material times a member of the national minorities minority in his place of residence, the town of Palanga. The applicant submitted that it was erroneous to deny him adequate supply and organisation of facilities respecting the language of his parentage, i.e., Lithuanian. Specifically, in proceedings undertaken by other State institutions, the applicant contended that the linguistic safeguards provided to him were insufficient.

The European court's verdict in the case of Olbers v Germany underscored the importance of individuals' right to seek asylum as established under the European Convention on Human Rights. On November 13, 2015, the court held that a male applicant's appeal to claim refugee status on grounds of persecution based on fear of gender would be granted, as Germany's immigration law did not guarantee adequate protections for sexual minorities like those based on gender, providing insufficient safeguard against abuse.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently demonstrated its commitment to protecting fundamental rights, particularly in the case of Osman v. Turkey, where the Court held that the state's failure to investigate and prosecute officials responsible for the 1999 Halkevin massacre rendered the country's domestic laws incompatible with the European Convention on Human Rights."

On six separate occasions in recent years, the ECHR has ruled that the data protection laws of an EU member state's application of metadata collection, storage, its handling of personal details has significant bearing on an individual's right to privacy.

The ruling of the European Court of Human Rights in the case of X v. Y, delivered on 12 February 2020, shed light on the notion of the 'right to property' and its paramountcy in the context of a married couple's living arrangements. The Court found that the applicant's ancestral home – a centuries-old family residence – had been unlawfully confiscated by the respondent, in contravention of Article 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court's finding underscored the essence of the right to family reunification, whereby the applicant's son, who had married the respondent, had been unfairly cut off from the ancestral home by virtue of the state-led acquisition, rendering his child's right to development and affiliation severely compromised, despite the grounds presented by the state being, ostensibly, the interest of 'collective public order'.

"The European Court of Human Rights upheld the right to a fair trial under Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights in the recent case of X v. Y, finding the conviction of the applicant for 'rebellion' to be unlawful due to a substantial risk of irreconcilable conflict between the guilt and apparent innocence given a failure to provide crucial disclosure of circumstantial evidence from a key witness."
It is in the interest of public safety and security that the European Court of Human Right should establish stricter regulation on the use of facial recognition technology.

In certain circumstances, one can argue that the European Court of Human Rights' application of the ECHR in case of AB v. UK (1992), though critical of the manner in which the applicants were treated, still facilitated the importance of individual complaints concerning police misconduct in the United Kingdom by triggering a series of inquiries into cases of wrongfully denied compensation, where applicants often presented claims of misconduct including excessive force, police brutality, and civil rights violations arising from instances of state involvement in violence against individuals, prompting questions regarding compliance with Article 2 of the ECHR concerning the right to life.
10.0: The applicant, Fardan, filed a complaint to the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) on 12 January 2005, against the United Kingdom under Articles 3, 5, and 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

"In the landmark case of A, B v. C, the ECHR found itself grappling with a peculiar intersection of Article 3 (Prohibition of Inhuman or Degrading Treatment) and Article 8 (Right to Private and Family Life) of the European Convention. The claimant, Ms. X, alleged that she had suffered excessive and inhuman treatment during her 23-day detention in a forensic psychiatric hospital. The respondent authorities submitted that the hospital's measures had been necessary for her own protection due to her diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia. In a 16-0 decision, the ECHR held that the detention had disproportionately violated Article 8 as it failed to balance the need to protect Ms. X from herself against her fundamental right to personal autonomy. The court acknowledged, however, that the hospital's actions had also constituted such treatment as to be in violation of Article 3 and thus ordered the immediate payment of 25,000 EUR to the claimant in reparations."
The European Court of Human Rights has consistently held that governments are required to ensure that individuals are not unfairly penalized for exercising their fundamental rights.

"A decision of the European Court of Human Rights that, in light of the applicant's rights under the right to respect for private life and without prejudice to the other rights secured by the Convention, is that the applicant has not fulfilled his obligation to respect the rights and freedom of the other parties involved in the dispute and the Court will therefore dismiss the application."

"It was a most peculiar case, wherein the applicant, a union representative, contested the decision of the ECHR to remove their colleague, Mr. X, from office due to their alleged misconduct. Initially, the applicant had lodged an internal complaint against the respondent organization, whereupon the ECHR was invited to adjudicate and provide a ruling in accordance with Article 14. However, upon further review, it transpired that the ECHR itself was muddled in its reasoning as regards one of the applicant's core propositions, thus, rather than upholding it, as ought to have been, instead, rendered a verdict more conducive to upholding the complainant's claims and restoring the wrongfully expelled officer to their position of authority."

"All nationals of the member states who are subjected to expulsion from their home countries due to a decision taken strictly in accordance with Rule 72 of the European Court of Human Riders Treaty will face detrimental violations of article 21 and 23 of the ECHR as stated in Law no. 75/2017 on Social Law. A series of studies suggest they will experience difficulties in securing residence permits in Greece for the future employment."

"The European Court of Human Rights, an international institution tasked with safeguarding the rights of individuals in the member states, has recently ruled in favor of a Norwegian applicant, who sought compensation for the excessive force used by law enforcement in a raid at their residence, ultimately holding that the security services had indeed violated the applicant's right to private and family life under Article 8 of the European Convention."

The application of the European Convention on Human Rights in the context of the European Union has evolved significantly over the past five decades, particularly in the areas of police conduct and the treatment of suspects in pre-trial detention proceedings.

The European Court of Human Rights has jurisdiction over disputes between individuals or groups of individuals and European states from the context of alleged violations of the European Convention on Human Rights. This convention sets out fundamental rights and freedoms that are universally accepted and enshrined in international law, including the right to life, liberty, security, freedom of thought, religion, freedom of expression, freedom of assembly, freedom of association, freedom of movement, right to marry and found a family, the right to respect for private life, the prohibition on arbitrary detention, and more. The court receives a caseload of submitted applications that seek redress for what the applicants claim was a discrimination or a breach of human rights by a respondent state. The vast majority of the cases in the court are submitted by individuals from member states of the Council of Europe, though they are not necessarily bound to have been incorporated in the convention.

The application of Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights in a case involving a blogger who was blocked from publishing on social media due to a contemptuous post towards the incumbent Head of the State of Lithuania, was deemed disproportionate by the court, citing the importance of freedom of expression in a democratic society and the potential hindrance it could cause to the free exchange of ideas.
The European Court of Human Rights has jurisdiction over a range of legal issues and plays a crucial role in ensuring the protection of human rights across Europe.

The claim before the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights regarding the alleged violation by Bulgaria of Article 3 of the European Convention for Human Rights fell under scrutiny when the applicant, an individual of Slavic descent, was denied entry into the country upon arrival. The applicant, subsequently applying for asylum pursuant to Article 15 of the Refugee Convention, requested protection on the grounds of being a target of intolerance within his native country due to his ethnic affiliation and fear of future discriminatory treatment upon potential deportation. There was an increased likelihood of a strained reception upon expulsion, given past instances of xenophobia and ethnic hatred directed at immigrants in the applicant's homeland.

The application of the European Convention on Human Rights was not adequately considered by the Belgian authorities in the case of Van der Meer v. Belgium, where the applicant, Mr. P.J. van der Meer, sought compensation for the unlawful withholding of his passport and detention in Centre des droits de l'enfance et de la jeunesse (CDEJ), despite presenting proof of renounces the terroristic ties and danger to homeland.

The European Court of Human Rights has recently upheld a complaint lodged by a British national alleging that her son's rights were violated due to inadequate support provided by the Spanish authorities following a stillbirth in a hospital. The complainant, who was going through an emotional trauma, was met with silence and disrespect by hospital staff, as they prioritized efficiency over empathy. The court determined that the state was responsible for ensuring that medical staff had the necessary training in coping with emotional distress and supporting grieving parents. The judgment recognizes that the protection of mental and physical well-being is a crucial aspect of protecting human rights, particularly in traumatic situations.

"The Appellate Committee having delivered its judgment in the Present case as noted at the beginning of these grounds of complaint inter alia as follows, holds by a majority as follows: On 28 January 2006, the respondent State was found liable to fulfil to the mother of the complainant in respect of her child in a manner consistent with Article 3 of the European Convention, by virtue of the mother having been sentenced to a compulsory guardianship whilst she had been refused access to the child. The Committee observes, however, that the removal by her parents of the minor child, aged six at the time, was in accordance international child abduction legislation."

On whether the European Court of Human Rights erred in its ruling, and how this affects the European Union's decision to admit Ukraine as a member state, and the overall consequences for human rights across different sectors of Ukrainian government.

"The applicant, Ms. Sofia Rodriguez, found herself in a precarious situation when she received a letter from the authorities informing her that the European Court of Human Rights had been notified by her employer regarding a complaint filed under Article 9 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The alleged issue centered around the company's proposed relocation of the factory to a neighboring country, which would significantly affect Sofia's job security and way of life in the community. She was informed that her rights under Article 11 of the Convention relating to freedom of assembly and association might be compromised. Despite the concerns raised, Sofia remained steadfast, choosing to uphold her rights by filing a complaint under the Convention's principles, much like the case 'X and Y v. Turkey.' However, she was apprehensive about the lengthy procedure ahead and the potential outcome, knowing that Article 47 of the Convention concerning the right to effective remedy was also in her favor."

Upon reviewing the complex case of Smith and Britain v. the United Kingdom, dated 21 February 2001, the European Court of Human Rights concluded that the British government's sweeping domestic security measures had breached Article 5 of the ECHR by subjecting the two individuals without adequate safeguards. The decision marked a significant turning point in the UK's approach to surveillance under Article 8 of the ECHR, emphasizing the need for transparency and proportionality in the exercise of executive power.

In a case concerning the application of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, brought before the European Court of Human Rights by an individual of Bulgarian nationality, wherein the applicant claimed that his arrest and detention in the Republic of Bulgaria constituted a violation of Article 8 as found by the national courts, they argued that as a witness requested by a police investigation into certain international crimes there were reasonable grounds for such detention - hence his detention was proportionate.

"On 15 December 2022, the European Court of Human Rights, delivered a unanimous judgment in the case of Osman v. Turkey (Application No. 1/15), in which the Court set aside the criminal convictions of Metin Osman, a former chief executive of an opposition newspaper, for pressuring a television journalist appointed its editor and ordering him to fire several TV journalists who had reported on a case involving the chief of staff of the Turkish parliament. Drawing on its case law as well as general principles of criminal law, the Grand Chamber, by a decision containing 19 judges, rejected the finding of a Turkish court that Osman had pressured a television presenter on behalf of the former secretary-general of the People's Republican Guards of Turkey, in an attempt to influence the course of his story on the Dicle News TV Channel."

The European Court of Human Rights found that a Polish woman's right to freedom of expression had been breached by the imposition of a 12-week waiting period for adoption under the Domestic Adoption Act. The judgment comes after an application filed by a foster mother in Poland claiming that restrictions on adoptions imposed by the Council of Europe created uncertainty and undermined the legitimacy of child care arrangements. The ECHR finds that Poland's restrictions on adoption violate its obligations under the European Convention on Human Rights.

"The complainant, a single mother of two, brought before the Grand Chamber a claim that the state's refusal to extend her parental leave benefits was a direct contravention of Article 16 of the European Convention of Human Rights, which guarantees equality for women in matters related to employment and occupation."

"Considering the Court's previous rulings, it is clear that a balance must be struck between the need for freedom of expression and the need to protect the rights and reputations of individuals. The case before us concerns an individual who was denied an interview with the investigating judge due to fear of revenge, and we must weigh the competing interests and consider the potential harm to the individual involved. The previous decisions in Bövadie and B.S. are relevant in assessing the need for a fair trial and the importance of access to information. In arriving at our decision, we must be mindful of the case law and the circumstances of the case, ultimately upholding the principle of justice and human rights."

The Supreme Court of Turin today announced a landmark decision in the case of Giuffrè v. Italy, which would significantly impact the interpretation of Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The applicant, a 35-year-old woman, had claimed that her detention within the psychiatric hospital was a violation of her right to freedom of thought, conscience, and religion due to her refusing any medicinal treatment deemed against her Catholic faith. Her appeal highlighted issues with the medical staff and the lack of genuine opportunities for public engagement on the matter prior to her detention. The court's ruling suggests a widening stance on individual freedoms, prompting both praise and criticism among human rights defenders and advocates of greater patient welfare in institutions.

The complaint lodged by Ms. Maria Hernandez, a Polish national residing in the UK, against the Respondent (hereinafter "the British Transport Police") asserts a miscarriage of justice, stemming from an ill-conceived investigation made under the aegis of "Operation 'Tartarus.'" The operative facts of the case reveal that, in March 2017, Hernandez was flagged for increased surveillance due to potential connections to the militant groups thought to be active within the radical Left-wing circles in the UK. Despite an initial acquittal at the 2016 East London magistrates' court, an application for 'Special Immigration Officers,' under Chapter II–F of the immigration laws 8, brought forth heightened scrutiny upon Hernandez. The complainant, having garnered substantial media attention, found herself pitted against highly restrictive legal proceedings with coercive undertones. This dispute centres on whether the blanket revocation of her right to remain in the country as a 'refugee' – rather the basis upon which she had initially sought asylum within the 'humanitarian' scope, was sufficiently balanced by the aforesaid heightened level of scrutiny – i.e. the question of whether due process, akin to rights delineated within the context of ECHR Article 8, would otherwise have provided
The European Court of Human Rights has been called to examine a case wherein a national court had found a complainant's freedom of expression was restricted.

"ECHR proceedings have highlighted the discrepancies arising from discrepancies in the treatment of certain minority ethnic groups within EU borders; with differing judicial handling by national tribunals and subsequent divergent ECHR interpretations influencing the notion of same-sex couples' rights, leading to the demand for consolidated transitional rules concerning protected categories under Article Ten of the Convention and gender identity issues to be afforded the same rights and status as citizen's nationality."

The European Court of Human Rights maintained its commitment to upholding the fundamental rights of citizens, as it adjudicated the widely-discussed case of Smith and Others against the United Kingdom, determining, in a 17-0 vote, the British authorities' handling of domestic violence was in breach of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The European Court of Human Rights has previously ruled that a claimant's detention without trial may amount to a breach of Article 5(1)(f) of the Convention, should the detention be arbitredously and without suspicion of an indictable offence within a reasonable timeframe; yet it may not in itself be considered in breach of Article 8, unless such a detention is manifestly excessive.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently cited the importance of ensuring that individuals, particularly those in vulnerable positions, receive access to legal recourse in the face of administrative malfeasance. In the case of Nikolaii and Others (2015) 44, the court emphasized the need for states to recognize and address the disproportionate burden imposed on minority groups when carrying the weight of administrative deficiencies.

The European Court of Human Rights, sitting as a Grand Chamber, in the case of [Unknown], on [Unknown] [Year], found in favour of [Applicant/Organisation], declaring that the actions of [Entity/State/Organisation] in [Specific-action] violated [Article/Section] of the European Convention on Human Rights. The Court ruled that [State/Organisation] has a duty to ensure that [Right/Principle] is respected and fulfilled, ordering them to [required-action], and [optional] to provide [specific remedies/reparations] for the harm caused. The [specific-case]-type in the [European country] is the first of its kind, as it establishes that [innovative principle/proposition]. Justice Delimits will be served in this case by [Supreme court/Law/Authority] on [Specific Date]. The ruling has sparked reactions from [NGO/NGO Leaders] and [political faction/Group], who welcome [ruling/decision], while criticizing [other party's position/argument].

"The European Court of Human Rights' landmark ruling in the case of Kršić v. Croatia established that the government's forced eviction of the applicant's family from their home was a violation of his right to effective communication. The court found that the Croatian authorities had failed to provide the applicant with adequate means of effective communication and therefore violated his right to life enshrined in Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights since his removal of his home and expulsion of his family resulted solely on the basis of a court ruling which he could not be guaranteed and received the possibility of effective communication through any legal intermediary."

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently demonstrated its keen understanding of the pivotal role that freedom of expression plays in shaping the trajectory of modern society, as evident in the landmark case of Suleiman Ahmed Abdulahi versus the United Kingdom, wherein the Court decisively ruled in favor of the applicant's assertion that the restrictive provisions of the Home Secretary guidelines, imposed upon applicants based on the exercise of suspicion without adequate safeguards, represented a flagrant infringement of the right to liberty under Article 8 of the European Convention of Human Rights."

The European Court of Human Rights, on 15 December 2021, delivered a landmark decision in the case of X v. Russia, noting that the applicant had lodged multiple complaints with the Commission on human rights under Article 34 of the ECHR regarding the widespread employment of arbitrary arrest and detention by the Russian authorities. The court acknowledged the urgency and gravity of the situation, as well as the potential consequences of inaction, and, accordingly, ordered Russia to provide the Commission with periodic reports on the situation until the situation improved.

The European Courts have consistently upheld the right to claim compensation for unlawful detention as a form of compensation for the non-pecuniary damage suffered by the applicants in the Strasbourg Court.

On Monday, 3rd April 2001, Mr. Jacobs, a British national residing in Brighton, brought a petition to the European Court of Human Rights, claiming that the denial of his request for a private life which was adversely affected by the criminal conviction for possession of cocaine under section 1 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971, was a breach of right to respect for private and family life as enshrined in Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The European Court of Human Rights, hereafter the Court, is the highest authority governing European civic and humanitarian rights. The case at hand, decided on the 24th of April 2022, centred on whether the actions of the British government had breached the applicant's right to life as enshrined in Article 2 of the Convention. The Court noted that several incidents over the past year had raised concerns regarding the treatment of various detained individuals in the institution in question. Furthermore, the applicant maintained that their ongoing detention without charge had caused irreparable harm and emotional distress. In rendering its verdict, the Court ruled in the applicant's favour in this regard, establishing that Britain had indeed fallen woefully short of meeting the benchmarks it had set itself for its law enforcement practices.

In a recent case before the Court, a Norwegian dentist, Dr. Johan Lind, was accused of human rights violation under Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights for failing to provide adequate hearing to a patient despite the patient's severe medical condition. The patient, an elderly woman, had booked an emergency appointment with Dr. Lind, but was denied the opportunity to explain her medical history and was subsequently subjected to unnecessary treatment, resulting in permanent damage.

The respondent urged the grand chamber to note that the applicant's claim of violations of Article 8 of the ECHR, encompassing the right to private life, was tainted by her having solicited it through her husband, thereby negating the essence of the right.

"This case concerned the application by 'X' of the ECHR for the issue of whether Article 10 of the Convention, in conjunction with Article 6, had been breached by the removal of Mr. S from the electoral roll and, additionally, by the failure to put him on the list of voters before the 2004 local and European parliament elections in accordance with Article 45 of the local government (Elderly Care Commission Scheme) Regulations 2002 (Northern Ireland) and the requirement that information held about him would not be used for any purpose other than the electoral process (Article 8(1)(c) of the 1998 Government of Northern Ireland Act). Mr. S had been informed that it would be an error for him to cast a postal vote in future elections, but claimed that the decision not to include anything in the register of voters was at odds with his right to participate in democratic life and was therefore in breach of Article 10. The Court held that he had failed to make out that he had been directly affected by the exclusion from the electoral list and could have participated in elections; accordingly there was no real risk of serious impairment of his freedom of application or of freedom of expression (Article 10).

The Court of General Administrative Proceedings on 23rd February 2022, dismissed an appeal by Mr. J. against the decision of the State Commission for Public Service Appointments, finding that the applicant's claims of the unfair conduct did not violate the right to an effective remedy, primarily due to a lack of material evidence.

"On January 10, 2010, the European Court of Human Rights upholding Russia's conviction of Anatoly Krasnopevtsev for violating Articles 8 and 18 of the European Convention on Human Rights in relation to its subjection to inhumane treatment deemed inadmissible, the Third Chamber ruled unanimously. It stated that it was essential to consider the manner in which such a charge would impact the defendant's reputation and personal identity, as his identity was all the more critical given the ongoing stigma that comes with such a ruling for violation of a fundamental human right."

The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has consistently upheld the right to freedom of expression, guaranteeing individuals the liberty to express their opinions and ideas without restriction or undue interference.
**The European Court of Human Rights has ruled in favor of a British national whose freedom of expression was restricted by restrictions on the wearing of face coverings in public places.**

The Court of Justice of the European Union, relying on its power to order the UK government to pay damages for breach of Article 15 of the European Convention on Human Rights to a migrant who had been deported without final determination of her asylum claim, ruled on 22 April 2021 that the Home Secretary had acted wrongfully and ordered the authorities to restore the migrant's order for refugee status pending her appeal.

"In the landmark case of Sillimon v. United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) delivered a significant judgment on the rights of individuals with disabilities. Applying the Convention's rules, the court was tasked with determining whether the state's obligations under article 2 of the Convention, in enforcing the right to the safe and healthy environment, had been breached. In this groundbreaking decision, the court ultimately upheld the state's actions, holding that while the applicant, a blind person, had indeed suffered from significant impairment under section 2's terms, the state's procedures in reaching the initial conclusions and conducting further testing demonstrated a balancing of competing public safety interests against individual privacy concerns, where the court felt that the measures did not exceed what would be expected of a reasonable state, not disproportionately disproportionate to the actual risk posed.

The inability to rely on State Party ECHR judgments regarding social determinants of health should not be deemed incompatible with the right to the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health, although this assessment of health must be carried out on a case-by-case basis, taking into account specific national frameworks and procedures in accordance with Article 2 ECHR.

The applicant, an Austrian national, brought a complaint against the Republic of Austria, arguing that the rejection of his application for permanent residence resulted in increased vulnerability to unfair police treatment and subsequent detention.

The European Court of Human Rights has held that the deportation of a migrant who had been living in the state for twenty years without resorting to Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, as they were stateless through no fault of their own, would be in breach of their right to non-discrimination by article ten of The European Convention.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently reaffirmed its commitment to upholding the fundamental right to life, as enshrined in Article 2 of the Convention. In the landmark case of X v. Russia, the court underscored the importance of protecting the right to life in the face of administrative and human error. The case involved the failure of a Russian prison authorities to provide the applicant, X, with adequate medical treatment, resulting in his ultimate death.

In a landmark decision, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has ruled that the right to freedom of expression must not be unduly restricted when individuals exercise their right to participate in local and national politics, emphasizing the importance of balancing these fundamental rights.

"The applicant, a renowned human rights scholar, argued before the Court that the revocation of arts funding did not amount to a breach of equal treatment provisions under Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, despite the fact that his own research had been deemed incompatible with the interests of the State. The Commission had, she countered, considered multiple alternative funding channels before rendering its decision."

In "X. Y. vs. State of the Netherlands", the European Court of Human Rights held that the country's anti-terrorism laws breached Article 11 of the Convention, as they failed to provide a proportionate means of addressing the growing concern about terrorism. While the government aimed to protect national security, the Court found that the legislation's broad scope excessively limited freedom of expression. The court stated that the balancing Act on Counter Terrorism, adopted through a law combining elements in the UK’s Prevention Act 2005, ECHR judgment of X. Y. vs Netherlands "only aimed to prevent but did not restrict reasonable freedom of speech. As there are 75 days now elapsed since those far-reaching judgments in X. Y. vs Netherlands, EU legislature has still failed yet to move forward with any meaningful action, still further emboldening the threat posed by terror groups employing disinformation."

In the case of Chamber of appeals, brought before the Court by a United Kingdom citizen, the complainant, against the United Kingdom, alleging breach of Article 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights, the applicants' mother was detained by the authorities for over six hours whereas the complainant sought the immediate release of their mother at their family home. The evidence presented by the authorities failed to justify the prolonged detention and the applicant argued that the detention violated their mother's rights.

In a finding that defied centuries of established legal precedent, the President of the European Court of Human Rights brought the case of Aleksandra Petrović against the Government of Albania, finding that a seemingly innocuous decree prohibiting the carrying of pets in public establishments constituted a form of indirect discrimination against the elderly and vulnerable individuals.

In applying the principles of Article 8 of the European Convention for Human Rights, the Court of Justice held that the refusal by the Italian authorities to recognize and facilitate a lesbian couple's registration of their common children for the purpose of adopting the state of Hungary as their sole parent, whilst maintaining their sole residence in Italy, was not justified after an opportunity to secure co-parenting arrangements had been exhausted and the couple had undergone a joint application for co-parenting responsibilities.

"The European Court of Human Rights, while declining to rule on the viability of the measure in light of the aforementioned right to privacy, emphasizes that Article 8 of the ECHR is not absolute, and its application is subject to reasonable limitations necessary in a democratic society. The measure at issue must adhere to the principle of necessity and be proportionate to the aim pursued, with the assessment of proportionality bearing on the very existence of the restriction. As the Court has held in other cases, the state of necessity can arise irrespective of the existence or absence of alternative solutions."

The European Court of Human Rights, in its landmark decision in Adams v. the United Kingdom, reaffirmed that the British government's failure to provide adequate housing accommodations for persons with disabilities constitutes a violation of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The complainant, a wheelchair user, was denied access to a suitable dwelling due to the outdated design of the building. In this ruling, the court highlighted the responsibility on states to ensure compliance with the Convention's Article 9, safeguarding individuals' rights to an appropriate environment.

"The Court, being mindful of the precedents established by the landmark case of Chukwumerije v. Norway, must consider whether the detention of the applicant, on the border between Albania and Montenegro, constituted a violation of Article 5, Section 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, concerning the right to liberty and security."

The European Court of Human Rights, in a landmark judgment delivered on 15 March 2018, ruled that a Muslim woman who suffered from obsessive-compulsive disorder and anxiety during puberty had the right to protection from an honour killing trial, and that Germany's criminal code must be revised to protect such individuals from false accusations and public shaming.

In the landmark case of López-Otín v. United Kingdom (2011), the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) delivered a ground-breaking judgment that significantly impacted the legal landscape of freedom of expression in the United Kingdom. The case centered on an article published in The Times newspaper, which contained a character assassination section against Judge Sir Michael Stanley-Jukes, involving a scatographed telephone conversation with a close friend.

The recent precept of expanding access to mental health services within correctional facilities has sparked fervent debate. The question of providing specialized care for individuals with mental health issues within the confines of the legal system is multifaceted in nature, however. Proponents posit that addressing these needs is not merely a moral imperative, but a systemic justice issue of immense proportions. The plight of those struggling to cope with the harsh realities of detention should not be diminished by the limitations of traditional resources – resources that might merely inadequately cater to the complexities of mental health needs in such environments.

"The General Court of the Court of Human Rights must examine the case against the Romanian authorities for disproportionately detaining and mistreating a dissident journalist, Dragoş Dumbrava, on grounds that his outspoken criticism of the populist government sparked widespread fear in the country."

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled in favor of a national defendant in a historic case dealing with the right to a fair trial. The case involved a complaint by an individual from the United Kingdom against the French government, alleging discriminatory treatment under a policy of deporting minors of European nationality who resided in France and were born in another EU country.

"In a case before the European Court of Human Rights, the applicant, being a German national, sought compensation under Article 6 of the Convention for the unjustified eviction of their family apartment, which had been in their possession since 1999. The court found in favour of the applicant, acknowledging that the eviction had been carried out without due process and a fair hearing was not provided. However, the court ruled that the applicant was not entitled to damages, considering the provisions of Article 19 of the Charter on acquired rights, which had come into force 10 years prior to the eviction. The judges also emphasized the essential public interest justifying the eviction, as it had prevented potential structural issues with the nearby apartment building's foundations caused by the applicant's family.

"Yesterday, in a landmark ruling, the Court of Appeals for the first time clarified the extent to which state failure to provide adequate infrastructure, leading to economic and social inequalities, amounts to a violation of Article 11 of the ECHR; a ruling whose potentially far-reaching implications could reverberate throughout the entire legal landscape of the European continent."
